content.text: Why User Testing Changes What Eval Suites Cannot

Automated eval suites test whether the model satisfies known criteria. User testing reveals criteria that were not known. The two methods are complementary: evals verify correctness at scale; user testing discovers gaps in the requirement set.
An eval suite run before user testing validates a spec that may be missing 20–30% of the requirements that real users will surface. User testing comes before finalising the eval suite, not after.
Three classes of findings consistently emerge from user testing that automated evals never surface:
Prompt literacy gaps
: users who cannot construct an effective first prompt reveal that the feature's onboarding is insufficient. This is invisible to automated evals because evals supply well-formed inputs.
Trust calibration errors

: users who over-trust or under-trust the model's output reveal that Claude's confidence signals (hedges, citations, disclaimers) are miscalibrated. Evals check whether hedges are present — they do not check whether users find them persuasive.
Interface metaphor mismatches
: users who treat the feature as a search engine, a chatbot, or a form reveal that the conversational interface is not meeting their mental model. This requires a UX change, not a prompt change.
Session Structure

attrs.alt: Rapid Prototype Testing Session Structure — 90-minute timeline with four phases: Warm-up (0-20 min), Prototype A (20-50 min), Prototype B (50-75 min), Debrief (75-90 min), with observation capture table

content.text: A standard rapid prototype testing session runs 90 minutes with one participant, comparing two prompt variants (Prototype A and Prototype B) on the same set of tasks. Five participants per round is the minimum for systemic findings — individual participant reactions are idiosyncratic; patterns across five are actionable.
Generating a Session Plan

The session plan specifies tasks derived from the behaviour spec must/must-not items, success criteria for each task, and interview questions for the debrief. Each task covers one scenario type from the conversation flow wireframe.

The must_not_trigger field in each task is critical — it specifies the model behaviour that would constitute a test failure if the participant inadvertently elicits it. For a legal review feature, this includes binding legal opinions and fabricated case citations.
Capturing and Structuring Observations

Facilitators take raw notes during sessions. Structuring those notes immediately after the session — while memory is fresh — produces higher-quality data than coding raw notes days later. The following pattern uses Claude to convert raw facilitator notes into structured observations, classified by type and severity.

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

OBSERVATION_TOOL = {
    "name": "record_observation",
    "description": "Record one observation from a prototype testing session.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "participant_id":  {"type": "string"},
            "task_id":         {"type": "string"},
            "prototype":       {"type": "string", "enum": ["A","B"]},
            "observation_type":{"type": "string", "enum": [
                "first_prompt_quality",
                "trust_signal",
                "correction_issued",
                "task_completion",
                "emotional_reaction",
                "unexpected_use"
            ]},
            "verbatim":        {"type": ["string", "null"]},
            "severity":        {"type": "string", "enum": ["critical","high","medium","low","positive"]},
            "linked_spec_item":{"type": ["string", "null"]}
        },
        "required": ["participant_id","task_id","prototype","observation_type","severity"]
    }
}

def synthesise_session_findings(raw_notes: str, participant_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Convert raw facilitator notes from a testing session into structured observations.
    Each observation is linked to a spec item where possible.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1500,
        tools=[OBSERVATION_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Participant: {participant_id}\n\n"
                f"Raw session notes:\n{raw_notes}\n\n"
                "Extract every distinct observation. For each: classify the observation type, "
                "include any verbatim quote, rate severity, and link to the spec item it exercises "
                "if identifiable. Record both problems (critical/high/medium/low) and positive findings."
            )
        }]
    )
    return [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "record_observation"]

# Example raw notes from a session
raw_notes = """
P03 opened Prototype A. First message: "Hi, can you help me with a contract?" — very vague, no clause text.
Claude asked a clarifying question. P03 said "oh, I need to give it the actual text" — pause, then pasted.
P03 re-read Claude's output twice before accepting the risk rating. Said "I don't know if I trust that."
P03 asked "is this enforceable?" — Claude gave a long hedged response. P03: "Why won't it just say yes or no?"
Switched to Prototype B — P03's first message included the clause text immediately.
On recovery task, P03 corrected a missed clause. B acknowledged and re-analysed. P03: "OK that's better."
Task 3 (boundary): P03 uploaded a 30-page doc. Prototype B warned about length. P03 appreciated the warning.
"""
observations = synthesise_session_findings(raw_notes, "P03")
for o in sorted(observations, key=lambda x: ["critical","high","medium","low","positive"].index(x["severity"])):
    print(f"[{o['severity'].upper():8}] {o['observation_type']:25} {o.get('verbatim','')[:50]}")
Six observation types cover the full range of findings relevant to Claude feature testing:
Observation Type
What It Captures
Primary Action
first_prompt_quality
User's unprompted opening message verbatim
Identifies onboarding gaps; update intake UX
trust_signal
Re-reads, push-backs, or unquestioned acceptance of Claude output
Miscalibrated trust → update hedges or citations
correction_issued
User identifies an error and corrects the model
Recovery failure → add recovery eval test
task_completion
Whether user achieved the task goal
Primary success metric for A/B comparison
emotional_reaction
Verbatim quotes of surprise, frustration, or delight
Updates persona emotion lane in journey map
unexpected_use
User attempts something the feature was not designed for
Scope creep risk → update scope classifier
Synthesising Findings Across Participants

Individual observations are data points. Cross-participant patterns are insights. An insight requires at least two participants to show the same behaviour — single-participant findings are noted but do not drive spec changes.

import anthropic, json
from collections import Counter

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

INSIGHT_TOOL = {
    "name": "extract_insight",
    "description": "Extract a testable insight from prototype testing observations.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "insight_id":       {"type": "string"},
            "pattern":          {"type": "string"},
            "prototype":        {"type": "string", "enum": ["A","B","both","neither"]},
            "participant_count":{"type": "integer"},
            "severity":         {"type": "string", "enum": ["critical","high","medium","low"]},
            "action": {
                "type": "string",
                "enum": [
                    "update_prompt",
                    "update_behaviour_spec",
                    "add_eval_test",
                    "update_journey_map",
                    "no_action_positive_finding"
                ]
            },
            "specific_change":  {"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": ["insight_id","pattern","prototype","participant_count","severity","action","specific_change"]
    }
}

def synthesise_cross_participant_insights(
    all_observations: list[dict],
    participant_count: int
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Synthesise observations from all participants into actionable insights.
    An insight requires ≥2 participants to be classified as systemic (not idiosyncratic).
    """
    obs_summary = json.dumps(all_observations[:30], indent=2)  # cap for context

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=2000,
        tools=[INSIGHT_TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "auto"},
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Total participants: {participant_count}\n\n"
                f"Observations (sample):\n{obs_summary}\n\n"
                "Identify patterns across participants. An insight is systemic if ≥2 participants "
                "show the same behaviour. For each insight, specify the exact change required: "
                "what to add/remove/change in the prompt, spec, eval suite, or journey map."
            )
        }]
    )
    return [b.input for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use" and b.name == "extract_insight"]

def prototype_winner(insights: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Tally which prototype performed better based on where critical/high issues appeared.
    """
    a_issues = sum(1 for i in insights if i["prototype"] == "A" and i["severity"] in ("critical","high"))
    b_issues = sum(1 for i in insights if i["prototype"] == "B" and i["severity"] in ("critical","high"))
    if a_issues < b_issues:
        return f"Prototype A wins: {a_issues} critical/high issues vs B's {b_issues}"
    elif b_issues < a_issues:
        return f"Prototype B wins: {b_issues} critical/high issues vs A's {a_issues}"
    return f"Tied: both have {a_issues} critical/high issues — decide on secondary criteria"

The action field on each insight specifies exactly what needs to change: update_prompt, update_behaviour_spec, add_eval_test, or update_journey_map. A round of five sessions should produce a prioritised list of insights, each with a specific change action, before the team moves to implementation.
Deriving Prompt Revisions from Insights

Insights that require prompt updates must produce specific clause-level changes — not generic recommendations. 'Make the model more cautious' is not actionable. 'Add this sentence to the system prompt' is.
Iteration Cadence

Rapid prototype testing runs in cycles. Each cycle is: author two prompt variants → run five sessions → synthesise insights → derive prompt revisions → update behaviour spec → run next cycle. Two or three cycles before implementation is the typical minimum for a zero-tolerance feature.
Cycle
Input
Output
Gate
Cycle 1
Two prompt variants from behaviour spec
Systemic findings; insight list; prototype winner
≥3 critical/high issues resolved before Cycle 2
Cycle 2
Revised winner prompt vs. new challenger
Reduced finding count; final prompt candidate
0 critical issues; ≤2 high issues
Cycle 3 (optional)
Final candidate vs. minimal prompt
Confidence in selected prompt
0 critical or high issues

A feature should not enter implementation until a prototype testing cycle returns zero critical findings and the selected prompt has been updated to address all high-severity insights. This is typically achievable in two cycles.
Summary

Rapid prototype testing with users surfaces three classes of requirements that automated evals cannot: prompt literacy gaps (onboarding failures), trust calibration errors (miscalibrated hedges and citations), and interface metaphor mismatches (UX and mental model issues). A standard session compares two prompt variants across a 90-minute protocol, capturing observations in six categories. Findings are synthesised into insights across five participants; insights drive specific prompt clause revisions, behaviour spec updates, new eval tests, and journey map updates. Two prototype testing cycles before implementation is the minimum for zero-tolerance features.
